sources.body:
## Summary

The cua-driver reference generator was `enabled: false` and pointed at `docs/content/docs/cua-driver/reference` (which does not exist), while the committed docs live at `docs/content/docs/reference/cua-driver`. So the runner/CI silently **skipped** it and falsely reported "up to date", and the reference drifted from the binary: committed said **v0.5.7 / 36 tools**, the shipping binary is **v0.7.0 / 38 tools** (missing `get_desktop_state` and `health_report`). This PR makes the generator the real, working owner of `cli-reference.mdx` + `mcp-tools.mdx`, preserves the hand-authored content, and wires release-time regeneration.

## What was repointed
- `cua-driver.ts` `DOCS_OUTPUT_DIR` and `config.json` `docsOutputPath` → `docs/content/docs/reference/cua-driver` (the one canonical location). No duplicate `docs/content/docs/cua-driver/reference` tree is created.

## Generator fixes for the current (Diátaxis) docs
Running the generator as-is would have **broken the docs build**: it emitted `import { VersionHeader } from '@/components/version-selector'` (that component no longer exists — no doc uses it) and `/cua-driver/reference/…` + `/cua-driver/guide/…` links that don't resolve.
- Dropped the dead `VersionHeader` block + `discoverVersions`/`VersionInfo` machinery.
- CLI intro now matches the repo convention: install snippet + "Documented against **{version}**" line.
- Fixed cross-links to `/reference/cua-driver/…` and `/explanation/process-model` / `/explanation/the-no-foreground-contract`.
- Backtick-styled command/tool headings to match the existing reference style.

## Curated content split out (nothing lost)
The per-tool bodies come from the binary's own `dump-docs` descriptions (rich and current). The genuinely cross-cutting hand-authored content — not derivable from any single tool schema — was extracted into two **non-generated** siblings:
- **`mcp-tool-notes.mdx`** ← "Common parameters", "Required parameters", "Platform-specific parameters", "Action response shape", plus the `get_window_state` degraded-result note and the `page` platform-support note (`trycua/cua#2084`).
- **`macos-permissions.mdx`** ← the macOS-only `cua-driver permissions` command (absent from `dump-docs`, so the generator can't emit it).

`meta.json` links both; the generated files link back to them. `contracts.mdx`, `limits.mdx`, `modality-test-suite.mdx` are untouched. Verified every curated phrase still resolves in the docs dir.

## Config / CI / script / workflow
- `config.json`: `enabled: false → true`; refreshed the stale note.
- `ci-check-docs.yml`: fixed the path filter and changed-generator grep (`docs/content/docs/cua-driver/reference` → `docs/content/docs/reference/cua-driver`) so the drift check actually runs for cua-driver instead of no-op passing.
- `docs/package.json`: added `docs:generate:cua-driver` (mirrors `docs:generate:lume`).
- **New** `cd-cua-driver-docs.yml`: on a `cua-driver-rs-v*` tag (or manual dispatch), rebuild + regenerate + open a PR with the refreshed MDX. No release workflow (lume included) previously regenerated docs, so this is a minimal tag-triggered workflow scoped to cua-driver, using the same App-token identity as the existing release automation.

## Regen result
- Categorized the five newer tools (`get_desktop_state`, `health_report`, `install_ffmpeg`, `start_session`, `end_session`) instead of dumping them in "Other tools".
- Regenerated to **v0.7.0 / 38 tools**; `get_desktop_state` and `health_report` now present.
- `npx tsx scripts/docs-generators/runner.ts --library cua-driver --check` now **actually checks** (builds, 38 tools) and reports up-to-date — no longer "Skipped (disabled)".
- Docs internal link check passes (0 errors).

## Known follow-up (intentionally out of scope)
CLI docs still come from the hand-maintained `cli_docs_json()` literal in `libs/cua-driver/rust/crates/cua-driver/src/cli.rs`. The CLI is hand-parsed (not `clap`), so there's nothing to introspect yet; adopting `clap` or reflecting the parser to make CLI docs source-derived (like the MCP side already is) is a separate change.

sources.body:
Adds an explicit **embedded mode** to cua-driver-rs so a host macOS app (an agent harness) can run the driver as a child process and have it inherit the host's Accessibility + Screen Recording grants — one permission prompt total, one entry in System Settings, no `com.trycua.driver` dialogs. Activated via `CUA_DRIVER_EMBEDDED=1` or `cua-driver mcp --embedded [--host-bundle-id <id>]`.

## Why

Standalone cua-driver deliberately makes itself its own TCC *responsible process* (disclaim re-exec + `open -a CuaDriver` relaunch) so grants attach to a stable bundle identity. That's right for standalone, wrong for embedding: a harness that already holds both grants ships a second app identity and its users hit a second set of permission prompts. macOS attributes TCC to the responsible process, so a directly-spawned child inherits the host's grants *for free* — the driver just has to stop opting out.

## What changed

**Behavior (~25 lines, all gated on the flag; standalone is unchanged when it's off):**
- `responsibility.rs` — skip the disclaim re-exec (stay in the host's chain). Decision factored into a pure `should_skip_disclaim()` seam for testability.
- `cli.rs` `should_use_daemon_proxy` — never auto-relaunch via the `CuaDriver.app` bundle (both platform branches; an embedded driver answers in-process).
- Permissions gate (`gate.rs`) — embedded mode opts out; the driver never raises its own prompts or panel.
- `check_permissions` — `prompt:true` is ignored (this + the gate are the only two `request_*` call sites, so prompts are unreachable), and `permission_source()` reports `attribution: "host"` with the advisory host bundle id and a `disclaim_env` debug field. The live `SCShareableContent` capture probe is untouched.

**Docs + example:**
- `Skills/cua-driver/EMBEDDING.md` — integration guide: TCC responsibility model, launch contract, standalone-vs-embedded table, `check_permissions` attribution semantics, copy-paste host example, troubleshooting (second prompt / black screenshot / empty AX / re-sign), Windows/Linux notes. Ships in the skill pack, so it links by repo path, not relative links.
- `examples/embedded-host-macos/` — `ExampleAgentHarness.swift`, a 122-line no-UI reference host mirrored **verbatim** in the guide (grants → embedded spawn → attribution check → background screenshot → background AX read → agent-cursor glide), plus a self-verifying `demo.sh` (build + sign + `tccutil reset` + tail-until-`DEMO COMPLETE: PASS`).
- `--embedded` / `--host-bundle-id` added to `--help`, `manifest`, and `dump-docs`; `cli-reference.mdx` regenerated via `scripts/docs-generators/cua-driver.ts` (no hand edits to generated docs).

## Security notes

- **The env var is caller-controlled, and that is safe by construction**: embedded mode only ever *removes* capability claims (suppresses prompting, downgrades attribution to `host`). It never feeds the `driver-daemon` attribution decision, which stays keyed on the non-spoofable bundle-path signal. New tests pin this, including embedded + disclaim env set together never yielding `driver-daemon`.
- Only the exact value `1` enables the mode (fail-safe); `--host-bundle-id` is an advisory label, not a trust signal — trust comes from the OS responsibility chain.
- The host must spawn the driver directly (`Process`/`posix_spawn`); launching via `open(1)`/`NSWorkspace` breaks inheritance. Documented, with the TCC `AttributionChain` log-stream command for debugging.

## Testing

- 20/20 unit tests pass (release profile): gate opt-out incl. exact-value rule, host attribution, fail-closed spoofing cases, disclaim truth table. Env-mutating tests across `gate` and `check_permissions` now share one crate-wide lock (they race under separate per-module mutexes).
- Example host + the guide's copy-paste block both compile with `swiftc`; verified byte-identical to each other.
- **End-to-end one-grant demo verified on a real Mac** (macOS Darwin 25.5, TCC reset via `tccutil`): host granted once, embedded driver started with zero driver-side prompts, `check_permissions` reported `attribution: host` with the live capture probe true, background Finder AX tree + window screenshot returned, agent-cursor overlay glided, `DEMO COMPLETE: PASS`. Settings listed only the harness — no CuaDriver entry. Still not CI-able (needs interactive grants).
- The manual run surfaced and fixed real issues: macOS 26 only registers an app in the Screen Recording pane after an actual ScreenCaptureKit attempt (the embedded driver's live probe is that attempt, attributed to the host — so the demo registers both rows in one pass); ad-hoc re-signing orphans grant rows (demo.sh now reuses the built bundle and skips `tccutil reset` on re-runs); `get_window_state` needs `pid`+`window_id` via `launch_app`; stock macOS has no `timeout(1)`.

## Not in this PR

- Nothing further is needed for Windows/Linux (X11) embedding: those platforms have no per-app permission grants, so a directly-spawned driver child inherits everything by plain process inheritance, and the flag already keeps it in-process. Two documented exceptions remain out of scope: driving elevated/UWP windows from an embedded host on Windows (embedded mode does not route through the uiAccess `cua-driver-uia` worker — the host must manage it), and Wayland capture (XDG portals prompt per-session and cannot be pre-granted; X11 is the supported path).
- CI automation of the TCC demo (requires interactive grant).



<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **New Features**
  * Added support for embedded mode in `cua-driver`, including a host bundle ID label and updated permission behavior for host-owned grants.
  * Added a macOS embedding guide and an end-to-end example host app for running the driver inside another app.
  * Expanded the macOS demo script to build, sign, run, and verify the embedded setup.

* **Documentation**
  * Updated CLI reference and README links to cover the new e…

sources.body:
## Summary

Fix Linux `launch_app` direct executable launches so they return launch identity in MCP `structuredContent`, not only in the human text response.

For direct executable launches, Linux now returns:

```json
{
  "pid": 12345,
  "bundle_id": null,
  "name": "sleep 30",
  "running": true,
  "active": false,
  "windows": []
}
```

## Why

Fixes #2090.

Before this change, Linux already had the child PID from `std::process::Command::spawn()`, but only emitted it in prose like `Launched '<cmd>' with pid <pid>.` macOS and Windows expose launch identity through structured content, so typed MCP clients can read the PID without parsing text.

## Scope

- Direct executable launches now attach structured launch identity.
- `xdg-open` fallback remains conservative and returns `pid: null`, because that helper may exit after spawning something else.
- URL-only launch behavior remains conservative and does not claim a target app PID.

## Verification

- Not run locally in this checkout. This machine is macOS, and a direct local `cargo check -p platform-linux` is not a valid Linux gate because Linux modules are cfg-gated. A Linux container check was attempted, but Docker daemon was unavailable locally.


<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **New Features**
  * On Linux, opening apps, paths, and URLs now returns richer structured results (not plain text).
  * Successful launches may include the process ID and an associated list of detected windows.
  * If a direct process launch fails, results now explicitly reflect that the system default handler (e.g., `xdg-open`) was used.

* **Bug Fixes**
  * Improved consistency of launch response shape across different open methods, including always returning the structured payload on success.
<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

sources.body:
## Problem

`list_apps` (and other tools) intermittently fail with:

```
MCP error -32603: daemon transport error forwarding `list_apps`: Resource temporarily unavailable (os error 35)
```

even though the request is tiny. Seen across a large fraction of trajectories; the heavy hitters are slow system queries (`list_apps`, `get_accessibility_tree`, `launch_app`).

## Root cause

`serve.rs::send_request` handles EAGAIN asymmetrically:

- **read side** (the response loop) already treats `WouldBlock`/`TimedOut` as "daemon still working, keep waiting" up to a 120 s deadline — the #1997 fix for #1864.
- **write side** had a 5 s `SO_SNDTIMEO` (`set_write_timeout`) but **no retry**: `w.write_all(...)?` propagates the timeout as a bare `os error 35`.

When the daemon (a machine-wide singleton serving every proxy) is momentarily too busy to read — backpressure from concurrent slow system tools — the proxy's write times out and the bare EAGAIN becomes a fatal transport error, wrapped by `proxy.rs` into the message above.

That `os error 35` has no `connect to …:` prefix (so not the connect path) and isn't the `timed out after 120s` read message — by elimination it's `write_all`, the only bare-EAGAIN site left after #1997 fixed the read side. A daemon momentarily not reading is the exact write-side mirror of a daemon still computing a response: not a transport failure.

## Fix

`cua-driver-core::socket_io::write_all_with_retry`: write with offset bookkeeping, treat `WouldBlock`/`TimedOut` as "keep waiting" until an overall deadline (the same 120 s budget as the read loop). `send_request` uses it instead of `write_all`. Extracted to `core` so the retry logic is unit-testable without linking the platform crates (and their Swift/Metal interop) the `cua-driver` binary pulls in.

## Tests

New `socket_io` tests:
- `retries_through_transient_eagain` — N transient `WouldBlock`s then success.
- `times_out_when_daemon_never_drains` — a never-draining writer surfaces `TimedOut` (not a bare EAGAIN) after the deadline.
- `accumulates_partial_writes` — offset bookkeeping for short writes.

## Verification status

- ✅ `cargo test -p cua-driver-core` green (3 socket_io tests + all core).
- ✅ `cargo check -p cua-driver` green.
- ⚠️ Not reproduced against a live backpressured daemon: the `cua-driver` binary doesn't link on my machine (unrelated platform-macos Swift/Metal interop vs the local SDK) and I can't re-run the eval that surfaced this. The retry logic is unit-tested and the `send_request` change is a drop-in for the existing `write_all`.


<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **Bug Fixes**
  * Improved socket writes to better handle temporary backpressure and partial writes.
  * Reduced failures caused by transient “resource temporarily unavailable” errors when communicating with the daemon.
  * Added a longer overall timeout so requests can keep retrying before failing.
  * Included validation to ensure writes complete reliably across retries and short writes.

<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
sources.title: fix(cua-driver): retry daemon socket writes on EAGAIN instead of failing fatally (write-side mirror of #1997)

sources.body:
## Summary

First landing PR extracted from #2161. It establishes the shared Rust E2E contract consumed by the platform follow-ups:

- typed case declarations, results, environment records, independent fixture journals, observers, sentinels, and trajectory evidence validation
- `cua-e2e-report` Markdown/matrix reporter and evidence-linking helper
- recording lifecycle and per-turn evidence capture improvements
- canonical cross-platform contracts and removal of superseded pre-typed modality tests
- shared fixture state needed by the Windows, macOS, and Linux PRs

This PR intentionally excludes platform implementations, platform workflows, support docs, and the independent Docker/TypeScript sidecars. The complete extraction/rollback plan is [documented on the frozen source branch](https://github.com/trycua/cua/blob/codex/link-e2e-videos/libs/cua-driver/docs/pr-2161-landing-plan.md).

## Landing adaptation

The original plan assigned final runtime wiring in `crates/cua-driver/src/main.rs` to this foundation. Native Linux and Windows CI proved that wiring depends on backend APIs owned by later platform PRs. It is therefore deferred and will be introduced as cfg-scoped platform wiring alongside those APIs, preserving this PR as a compile-complete shared foundation.

## Validation

- `cargo test -p cua-driver-testkit -p cua-driver-core` (157 tests passed)
- loopback journal test passed 30 consecutive runs after fixing accepted-socket nonblocking behavior
- `cargo run -p cua-driver-testkit --bin cua-e2e-report -- --help`
- focused Clippy passed with allowances only for identified pre-existing lints in untouched core files
- `bash -n` on the evidence linker and changed install/sync scripts
- preservation-queue exclusion audit passed

## Known baseline gates

On macOS, full `cargo check --workspace` currently reaches an unchanged `platform-linux` host-cfg error (`health_report.rs` references the Linux-gated Wayland module). Repository-wide rustfmt/clippy also report extensive pre-existing findings in untouched files. Native CI remains the authoritative full-workspace gate.

Source: #2161.
sources.title: test(cua-driver): typed e2e evidence foundation (conv2 1/7)

sources.body:
## Summary

Combined Linux convergence landing PR extracted from #2161, built on the typed evidence foundation and shared fixture correction.

The original plan separated Linux core/X11 (4/7) from Wayland (5/7). Native compile evidence showed that the input, health-report, recording, and window-dispatch APIs form one platform contract: landing only the X11 half produced missing Wayland symbols and signature mismatches. This PR therefore combines those two boundaries instead of adding temporary compatibility shims.

- converges AT-SPI discovery/listening, Linux input delivery, X11 window/input paths, overlay behavior, health reporting, and recording hooks
- lands the complete Wayland backend: ext-toplevel, Sway IPC, portal screenshot, persistent virtual pointer/libei paths, target activation, and Wayland recording
- splits portal features into `portal-input` and `portal-capture`, while preserving `portal-libei` compatibility
- replaces old modality tests with typed GTK3, Electron, Tauri, capture, and desktop-scope harness coverage
- makes the X11 and Wayland scripts/workflows canonical and retires the superseded interactive Linux workflow
- updates the GNOME helper and release-build wiring needed by the platform implementation

Nested compositor/Nix packaging remains in the next PR. Final support/roadmap docs remain in the capstone PR. The extraction plan is [documented on the frozen source branch](https://github.com/trycua/cua/blob/codex/link-e2e-videos/libs/cua-driver/docs/pr-2161-landing-plan.md).

## What this fixes

- preserves stable compositor identities across Wayland connections instead of treating connection-local object IDs as durable window IDs
- reconciles AT-SPI frame geometry with current Sway placement, so a launch-time `(120,120)` observation cannot shift later pixel actions after the compositor moves a window
- reconstructs roleless WebKitGTK WebProcess bounds from the compositor and embedded document rather than landing pointer actions one server-decoration bar above the target
- refreshes live Wayland fixture geometry for each snapshot, matching the native GTK harness and real clients that re-read moving-window state
- uses target-aware native Wayland foreground pointer/keyboard routes and typed background refusals where stock Wayland offers no target-addressed delivery protocol
- routes WebKitGTK foreground scroll through a real Wayland wheel when AT-SPI acknowledges the request without changing page state
- keeps X11 background delivery honest: supported AT-SPI/XSendEvent routes deliver, while unsupported raw/focus-bound shapes return exact refusals without changing focus, z-order, cursor, or unrelated input

## Validation

Exact source: `72083bb480c0281cc3186f06ac66c2b62d714e8e`

- [Linux X11 canonical run 29213365684](https://github.com/trycua/cua/actions/runs/29213365684): all lanes and matrix summary passed
  - shared Electron + Tauri: 42 delivered, 30 exact refusals
  - native GTK3: 24 delivered, 7 exact refusals
  - capture + desktop scope: 5 delivered
  - total: **71 delivered, 37 exact refusals, 0 failed, 0 skipped across 108 rows**
- [Linux Sway canonical run 29213349962](https://github.com/trycua/cua/actions/runs/29213349962): all lanes and matrix summary passed
  - shared Electron + Tauri: 42 delivered, 30 exact refusals
  - native GTK3: 24 delivered, 7 exact refusals
  - capture + desktop scope: 5 delivered
  - total: **71 delivered, 37 exact refusals, 0 failed, 0 skipped across 108 rows**
- every row carries structured case/result data, trajectory JSON, and an owned MP4
- Rust Linux/Windows compile and unit tests, Nix source, distro compatibility, docs sync, release reminder, and SPDX checks passed on the same head

Source: #2161.

sources.body:
## Summary

- Add default-on, consent-aware telemetry to Cua Driver and Lume with persistent disable controls and explicit reset and purge behavior.
- Send a content-free schema v2 envelope to PostHog EU with person profiles and GeoIP enrichment disabled.
- Record bounded CLI, MCP session, MCP tool outcome, install, release, and Lume usage events.
- Accept optional MCP agent context only through the documented `_meta["ai.cua/agent-context"]` object, then normalize provider and model values through fixed allowlists.
- Serialize installation identity and lifecycle state across processes, preserve returning identities during legacy-home migration, and retry failed lifecycle delivery after a 15-minute backoff.
- Persist installer attribution before binary activation so an immediate first command cannot mislabel an install as `first_run`.
- Publish the exact Cua Driver event schema and privacy boundaries.

## Privacy and user impact

A normal uninstall preserves the pseudonymous installation ID and saved preference so returning installations remain measurable. Disabling telemetry stops all telemetry requests. A first run with telemetry disabled creates no ID or markers. Reset and purge commands delete canonical and migratable legacy identity state.

Routine events exclude prompts, tool arguments and bodies, typed text, screenshots, accessibility trees, titles, application names, file paths, URLs, arbitrary metadata, and raw errors.

## Coordinated rollout

Cloud dashboards and GitHub collector: https://github.com/trycua/cloud/pull/5670

Apply the Cloud PR before releasing these clients. The trajectory-donation work remains separate.

## Checks

- `cargo test -p cua-driver --bin cua-driver --locked` (80 tests)
- `cargo test -p cua-driver-core --locked` (128 unit tests and 3 integration tests)
- `cargo check -p cua-driver --all-targets --locked`
- Focused telemetry tests (20), including multi-process identity/lifecycle, legacy reset, retry, and installer-attribution coverage
- Release wiring tests (10)
- Lume telemetry tests (6) and version-check tests (2)
- Python wrapper tests (3 passed, 3 platform skips)
- Shell syntax, documentation formatting, and git whitespace checks
- Independent privacy, concurrency, migration, and correctness review

## Summary

Final replacement PR from the #2161 second-wave convergence landing.

- documents the two-layer Rust test model: deterministic unit/protocol tests and real-application Harness E2E
- publishes current Windows, macOS, Linux X11, Sway, GNOME, KDE, and nested-compositor support boundaries using the Diataxis split between explanation, reference, and contributor guidance
- records the accepted exact-source baselines: Windows 114/114, macOS 133/133, Linux X11 108/108, and Linux Sway 108/108
- documents AX/PX targeting, foreground/background delivery, exact refusals, cross-cutting desktop oracles, typed row evidence, and per-cell MP4/trajectory ownership
- wires Windows, logged-in macOS, Linux X11, and Linux Sway validation into the cua-driver release reminder
- removes obsolete Python/shell/GIF fixture suites and historical plans after their useful contracts were mapped to typed Rust owners
- refreshes generated Lume references from current 0.3.15 source after the post-rebase generator gate found the 0.3.14 pages stale

This PR preserves the existing user-authored documentation edits that accumulated on the convergence branch; it does not quarantine or discard them. It is rebased onto merged #2184 and contains only the final docs/release-wiring/cleanup slice plus the explicit Lume generator adaptation.

## Important contracts documented

- A successful tool response does not prove delivery; fixture or desktop state must change.
- Background rows attach focus, z-order, cursor, and leaked-input oracles where applicable.
- Unsupported routes pass only when the exact declared structured refusal and side-effect contract are observed.
- Canonical contributors run one selector-free `all` command per OS; shared/native/capture fan-out is internal CI plumbing.
- Calculator and TextEdit are supporting typed checks in the canonical logged-in macOS lane. LibreOffice remains optional.
- Sway's complete declared Electron, Tauri, GTK3, capture, and desktop-scope catalog is accepted; arbitrary raw background input is still not claimed for stock Wayland.
- The private nested `cua-compositor` remains experimental and is not evidence of a stock-Wayland capability.
- Strict evidence normally requires before/after state and images. The narrow Windows minimized-window restore exception still requires successful `bring_to_front`, captured post-action accessibility state, explicit classification, trajectory, and finalized MP4.

## Cleanup

Removed obsolete owners under:

- `libs/cua-driver/tests/fixtures/linux-container/**`
- `libs/cua-driver/tests/fixtures/modality-recordings/**`
- `libs/cua-driver/tests/fixtures/vision-agent-test/**`

sources.title: ci(cua-driver): publish desktop e2e matrix results

sources.body:
## Summary

This PR strengthens the Rust desktop E2E contract and fixes the product defects that the new oracles exposed.

### Driver fixes

- Windows UIA actions now scroll fully clipped controls into view before acting and keep the UIA off-screen state in sync.
- Windows background `type_text` through `ValuePattern` now appends to the control's existing value instead of replacing it.
- macOS keyboard delivery clears inherited modifier flags before posting text and key events, preventing stale modifiers from changing later input.

### E2E signal

- Background delivery requires the target to be fully covered, with stable focus, z-order, foreground input, and cursor position where the platform exposes it.
- The foreground sentinel records focus, visibility, keyboard, pointer, wheel, and heartbeat events.
- Preflight deliberately leaks input and raises the target to prove that the guards can detect both failures before product rows run.
- Shared Electron, Tauri, and WKWebView catalogs now contain 40 typed cells per harness, including AX/PX, foreground/background, exact type-then-Return workflows, editor save, and scroll state.
- Fixture state checks use exact journaled outcomes instead of trusting a successful driver response.
- Canonical runs reject zero-cell filters, unexpected skips, under-sized catalogs, ambiguous window identity, and orphaned videos.
- Sway observation resolves the intended container, handles nested fullscreen ancestors, and refuses ambiguous PID-only matches.
- Preflight owns fixture child cleanup even when activation fails.
- Contributor docs describe the matrix, evidence contract, reports, and canonical commands.

## Why

A successful driver response does not prove that the target application received an action. Background delivery also needs evidence that the target was covered and that the user's foreground remained unchanged. These changes make those claims explicit and machine-checked, then fix the driver behavior that failed the stronger checks.

## Validation

Exact final head: `64e824490b6a796102a46fcece518a9d6c32c145`

- [Windows complete matrix](https://github.com/trycua/cua/actions/runs/29257963004): native WPF/WinUI3/WebView2, capture/desktop scope, shared Electron/Tauri, and combined summary all passed.
- [Wayland shared matrix](https://github.com/trycua/cua/actions/runs/29257961614): Sway shared matrix and typed summary passed.
- [Linux X11 complete matrix](https://github.com/trycua/cua/actions/runs/29254936043): capture 5/5, native 31/31, and shared 80/80 passed at `268a6ab1`. Later commits only change preflight identity and cleanup logic for Windows/Sway.
- macOS local canonical matrix: 138 deliveries and 6 expected refusals passed. One AppKit background AX click was interrupted by physical cursor movement; the strict cursor guard caught it. The same row then passed in isolation on final head with fixture-state, focus, z-order, cursor, and no-leaked-input evidence. Final-head daemon, AX, screenshot, canary, and video preflight also passed.
- `cargo test -p cua-driver-testkit --lib`: 40 passed.
- `cargo test -p platform-macos --lib`: 121 passed.
- Changed Rust files pass `rustfmt --check`; the branch passes `git diff --check`.

## Scope

This PR changes the Rust driver, repo-owned fixtures, E2E testkit, canonical runners, and contributor documentation. It does not import an external Python harness or add model-driven tests.

## Known limitations

- Standard Wayland does not expose the physical pointer position, so Wayland rows cannot claim the cursor-preservation oracle.
- Installed LibreOffice coverage remains an optional external-app suite outside the canonical run-all command.
- Expected background refusals remain visible as typed matrix outcomes; they are capability limits, not claimed deliveries.

sources.body:
## Summary

- allow `page.execute_javascript` to accept explicit `cdp_port` and `target_url_contains`
- require an explicit target hint to match exactly one CDP page
- reject invalid CDP ports instead of truncating them

## Why

Multi-window Electron processes expose multiple page targets on one CDP port. `execute_javascript` previously discarded the caller's resolved page identity and selected the first page target. An explicit URL hint also fell back to the first page when no target matched, so a successful RPC could execute against the wrong renderer.

The lower-level CDP session code already supported selecting a target by URL. This change threads that identity through the `page` tool and makes explicit hints fail closed when absent or ambiguous.

## Validation on latest upstream main

Rebased without conflict onto `trycua/cua@8c921b2b3bf13494724ead4f0a814d80c56a7e8b`.

```text
cargo test -p cua-driver-core page::tests
  3 passed

cargo test -p platform-macos browser::cdp_client::tests
  6 passed

cargo test -p cua-driver-core cdp::tests
  2 passed

cargo check -p cua-driver --release
  passed

cargo check -p platform-windows --target x86_64-pc-windows-gnu
  passed

cargo check -p platform-linux  # Debian bookworm / Rust 1.92
  passed
```

The targeted-page commit is `68a42c06bf3fdfa429d65e9c011efa13c2414548`; review feedback is addressed by `5c2b402d`, which reuses the existing CDP session cache, and `0e9a8294`, which prevents replay after a script has been sent. `35fa5658` completes explicit targeted routing on Windows and Linux. `git range-diff` reports it equivalent to the originally tested patch commit `adef3e87405986cc82df52ae59aef4c32e08a082`.

## Real multi-window downstream validation

Maka uses cua-driver as the sole page executor. Its real-machine harness runs two independent Electron `BrowserWindow` pages on one CDP port and passes the exact port + unique URL hint through cua-driver for pointer actions, element inspection, `Input.insertText`, and post-action readback.

Final batch `semantic-targeting-v5`:

- 10/10 complete runs passed
- 39/39 checks passed in every run
- two-window click/type routing and text isolation passed in every run
- zero fallback, wrong-target, no-op, or duplicate-effect cases
- frontmost application and real pointer remained unchanged

Per-action results:

| Action | Pass | Route | p50 / p90 / max |
| --- | ---: | --- | ---: |
| left click | 10/10 | targeted `page.execute_javascript` | 84 / 87 / 102 ms |
| checkbox click | 10/10 | targeted `page.execute_javascript` | 70 / 77 / 77 ms |
| range drag | 10/10 | targeted `page.execute_javascript` | 96 / 102 / 127 ms |
| right click | 10/10 | targeted `page.execute_javascript` | 74 / 80 / 85 ms |
| double click | 10/10 | targeted `page.execute_javascript` | 74 / 78 / 98 ms |

The downstream repo also passed its full typecheck, build, tests, bundle provenance gate, and final 39/39 real-machine E2E after integrating the patched driver.

## Compatibility

- Repeated targeted evaluations reuse the existing per-port CDP session cache, avoiding repeated Chrome remote-debugging confirmation prompts.
- Retry is limited to retarget failures before `Runtime.evaluate`; a sent script is never replayed after timeout or disconnect.
- Windows and Linux consume explicit `cdp_port` and unique URL hints through the shared CDP helper; calls without explicit targeting retain the existing backend behavior.
- Backends that do not implement targeted execution fail closed only when the caller supplies an explicit target.
- `insert_text` and `type_keystrokes` now share the checked CDP port parser, eliminating the previous `u16` truncation behavior.
sources.title: fix(cua-driver): target page JavaScript exactly

## Summary

- correct daemon lifecycle telemetry across macOS permission and responsibility re-execs
- replace the ambiguous MCP entry event with a bounded startup outcome
- observe MCP-over-HTTP tool completions with the existing content-free classifier
- add allowlisted tool names to finite CLI completion events
- add started, dismissed, and completed events for the macOS permission gate

## Privacy

All new fields are booleans, fixed enums, or existing duration/output buckets. The events do not include tool arguments or results, typed text, screenshots, accessibility data, application or window names, URLs, paths, socket names, raw errors, or stack traces. Telemetry opt-out still short-circuits before installation identity creation.

## Validation

Validated on commit `7ac5836714e99f945e13e840d400f0a637edf56d`:

- `cargo check -p cua-driver --locked`
- `cargo test -p cua-driver --bin cua-driver --locked` (87 passed)

- `cargo test -p cua-driver --test protocol_tools_call_test --locked` (14 passed)
- `git diff --check`

Workspace-wide `cargo fmt --check` is not included because the current workspace contains unrelated pre-existing formatting differences. This PR does not reformat unrelated files.

## Scope

This PR contains the event and transport contracts required by the first telemetry funnel. Dashboard configuration, documentation, and additional session-level analytics will follow separately.

sources.body:
This pull request introduces comprehensive documentation and workflow updates for Cua Driver’s permission policy system, including new conceptual and reference guides, a practical how-to, and CI workflow enhancements. The documentation now covers how permission policies work, their YAML and Rego formats, evaluation order, and usage instructions. Additionally, the CI workflow is updated to test both YAML and Rego policy VMs, and documentation references are updated for Cua Driver version 0.8.2.

**Documentation: Concepts and Reference**
- Added a new concept guide, `how-permission-policies-work.mdx`, explaining the structure, enforcement, evaluation, and trust model of the permission policy engine.
- Introduced a reference page, `permission-policies.mdx`, detailing the YAML schema, Rego interface, environment variable, evaluation order, and denial responses for permission policies.
- Updated `meta.json` files to include the new concept and reference pages in navigation. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-0e8f7456053ba88b6e6c0e1caa8597e5a8325d28e827a71987b1eac938d8286bL1-R1) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-f1d2952ceaf61bec20fde4c9752ff84f80427ff69839989bba2e48eb64644fddR11)

**Documentation: How-to Guides**
- Added a new step-by-step guide, `restrict-tool-access.mdx`, showing how to create, enable, and troubleshoot YAML and Rego permission policies, including argument constraints and environment variable setup.
- Updated the how-to guide navigation to include the new policy restriction guide.

**CI Workflow**
- Updated `.github/workflows/ci-nix-linux.yml` to add jobs for testing the YAML and Rego policy VMs, ensuring policy engine coverage in CI.

**Version and Reference Updates**
- Updated documentation references to Cua Driver version 0.8.2 in CLI and MCP tool docs. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-ce0092f54132105abc7e323867ba0849eaf48dec78b6f050e85b2372a08f392dL10-R10) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-ce0092f54132105abc7e323867ba0849eaf48dec78b6f050e85b2372a08f392dL19-R19) [[3]](diffhunk://#diff-05e11b4afd073cef624d913b2c694b32b88e40ef01b91fa109f398036512c826L10-R10)

These changes significantly improve the discoverability, usability, and test coverage of the permission policy system.

This PR improves the disk image reassembly process to reduce both memory usage and storage requirements:
- Reduced storage requirements: Now reads directly from cached image parts instead of making temporary copies, reducing storage needs from 3x to just over 1x the image size
- Memory optimization: Implemented adaptive chunk sizing based on available system memory
- Reduced log noise: Limited progress reporting to 5% increments
- Enhanced stability: Added special handling for memory-constrained systems with more aggressive resource cleanup

These changes allow large images (60GB+) to be reassembled on systems with limited RAM and storage.

sources.body:
This PR improves lume with support for multiple VM locations and configurable cache settings, improving flexibility and resource management.


### Multiple VM Locations
- Added ability to create and manage VMs in different storage locations
- Introduced commands for VM location management:
  - `lume config location add <name> <path>` - Add a new location
  - `lume config location remove <name>` - Remove a location
  - `lume config location list` - List all locations
  - `lume config location default <name>` - Set default location
- Added `--location` parameter to key commands:
  - `lume create` - Create VMs in specific locations
  - `lume pull` - Pull images to specific locations
  - `lume run` - Run VMs from specific locations

### Configurable Cache Directory
- Added ability to set custom cache location for VM images
- Introduced cache configuration commands:
  - `lume config cache get` - Show current cache directory
  - `lume config cache set <path>` - Set new cache directory
- Removed `--no-cache` parameter in favor of configurable cache location

### XDG Base Directory Compliance
- Moved configuration to `~/.config/lume/config.json` following XDG spec
- Implemented automatic migration from legacy settings

## Technical Improvements
- Enhanced VM management to track and respect location context
- Improved error handling for location-specific operations
- Added detailed logging for VM location operations
- Simplified caching behavior through centralized configuration

## Migration
Existing VMs will continue to work as before, with automatic migration of settings to the new configuration format.
sources.title: [Lume] Add multiple VM locations and configurable cache

sources.body:
# Implement sparse file support and push command

## Overview
This PR adds proper sparse file support for VM disk images and introduces a new push command for sharing VMs. It significantly reduces disk space usage while ensuring reliable VM bootability regardless of cache settings.

- **Sparse File Support**: Dramatically reduces actual disk usage while maintaining full functionality
- **Push Command**: Enables sharing VMs via the registry with proper partitioning
- **Reliable Partitioning**: Fixes boot issues by properly preserving partition tables

```bash
lume push <name> <image:tag> --additional-tags 'tags'
```
sources.title: [Lume] Sparse Push & Pull Optimizations

This PR introduces **Lumier**, a Docker-based interface for the lume CLI that simplifies running macOS virtual machines in containerized environments:

- Creates a secure tunnel to execute lume commands from container to host
- Provides easy VM management with a simple CLI interface
- Offers VNC access to macOS VMs through browser (port 8006)
- Supports customizable VM configurations (CPU, RAM, display resolution)
- Includes flexible shared storage mounting between host and container

**Lumier** complements our existing VM management tools while providing containerization benefits for improved portability and isolation.

This PR introduces necessary changes to the `Computer` interface to support multiple `VMProviderType`s, including Lume, Lumier, Cloud.

**Changes Included:**

* Added initial Linux provider for a CUA-compatible ARM64 Ubuntu image.
* Changed Lume daemon service port from `3000` to `7777` to prevent conflicts with common frontend development ports.
* Refactored the `Computer` Python package to use optional dependencies.
* Removed `pylume` dependency in favor of direct HTTP API calls.
* Improved Lume disk caching: `disk.img` is now cached as a fully reassembled image rather than in parts, enabling faster cache hits.

sources.body:
## Summary

Fixed the Lume macOS installation script failing with 404 errors due to downloading from the wrong GitHub release.

## Problem

The install script at `libs/lume/scripts/install.sh` was using `/releases/latest` which resolves to the most recent release across ALL components (currently `cua-v0.1.7` - a CLI release). This caused 404 errors when trying to download lume-specific files:

- Expected: `lume.tar.gz` from `lume-v0.2.22` ✅
- Actual: Trying to download from `cua-v0.1.7` ❌ (doesn't have lume files)

## Solution

Implemented proper release detection using GitHub's `/tags` API:

1. **Query `/tags` endpoint** instead of `/releases/latest`
2. **Filter for lume releases**
   - Grep pattern: `'"name": "lume-'` to match only tags starting with `lume-`
   - Excludes false matches like `pylume-v0.2.1`
3. **Use specific release tag in download URLs**
   - Before: `https://github.com/trycua/cua/releases/latest/download/lume.tar.gz`
   - After: `https://github.com/trycua/cua/releases/download/lume-v0.2.22/lume.tar.gz`

## Files Changed

- `libs/lume/scripts/install.sh`

---

Fixes #583
sources.title: fix(lume): Resolve install script downloading wrong release

## Summary
Reduce Login Items notifications from 2 to 1 by integrating the updater into the daemon.

## Problem
macOS shows two separate "App Background Activity" notifications:
1. "Software from Cua AI, Inc." (lume daemon)
2. "lume-update" (auto-updater)

## Solution
- Create a daemon wrapper script (`lume-daemon`) that:
  1. Runs the updater in background at startup
  2. Then runs the lume daemon
- Use only one LaunchAgent that runs this wrapper
- Remove the separate updater LaunchAgent
- Clean up legacy updater LaunchAgent on reinstall

Only one Login Item notification: "Software from Cua AI, Inc."

## Test plan

2. Verify only one Login Item appears in System Settings
3. Verify daemon starts and updater runs (check /tmp/lume_updater.log)

sources.body:
## Summary

This PR adds telemetry tracking, CLI improvements, comprehensive documentation rewrite, and build script enhancements to Lume.

### Telemetry
- Add PostHog telemetry via direct HTTP API (no SDK dependency - the iOS SDK crashed in CLI context)
- Track all CLI commands: `create`, `run`, `stop`, `delete`, `clone`, `pull`, `push`, `serve`, `setup`
- Track all HTTP API endpoints for VM operations
- **Installation event**: Sent once on first run, regardless of opt-out setting (to track adoption)
- **Opt-out**: Via `lume config telemetry disable` or `LUME_TELEMETRY_ENABLED=off` env var
- **Debug mode**: `LUME_TELEMETRY_DEBUG=on` to see telemetry logs
- Uses same PostHog API key as Python SDKs

### CLI Improvements
- Add ASCII art banner displayed on `lume` or `lume --help`
- Add `lume config telemetry` subcommand with `enable`, `disable`, `status` actions

### Build Scripts
- Make all build scripts location-independent (can run from any directory)
- Add `uninstall.sh` script with `--purge` (remove all data) and `--force` (no prompts) options
- Add ASCII art branding to install.sh and uninstall.sh

### Documentation Rewrite
- **Quickstart**: Add IPSW download options (manual vs `--ipsw latest`)
- **Introduction**: Clearer overview of Lume capabilities
- **Installation**: Updated installation instructions
- **VM Management**: Comprehensive guide to VM operations
- **Unattended Setup**: Improved automation documentation
- **HTTP Server**: Better API documentation
- **FAQ**: Updated frequently asked questions
- **Lumier (Docker)**: Improved Docker deployment docs
- Add architecture diagram (`lume-architecture.png`)
- Add Claude Cowork sandbox example
- Reorganize CUABench examples section

### Other Changes
- Add `sequoia.yml` unattended preset
- Minor fixes in CommandDocExtractor and VMDetailsPrinter
- Update CopilotKit provider configuration

## Changed Files

### Telemetry & CLI
| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `libs/lume/src/Telemetry/TelemetryClient.swift` | **New** - PostHog HTTP API telemetry client |
| `libs/lume/src/Main.swift` | ASCII banner, telemetry init on startup |
| `libs/lume/src/Commands/Config.swift` | Add telemetry subcommand |
| `libs/lume/src/Commands/*.swift` | Add telemetry calls to all commands |
| `libs/lume/src/Server/Handlers.swift` | Add telemetry to HTTP API handlers |
| `libs/lume/src/FileSystem/Settings.swift` | Add telemetryEnabled setting |
| `libs/lume/src/LumeController.swift` | Add telemetry management methods |

### Build & Install
| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `libs/lume/scripts/uninstall.sh` | **New** - Uninstall script |
| `libs/lume/scripts/build/*.sh` | Location-independent paths |
| `libs/lume/scripts/install.sh` | Add ASCII art |

### Documentation
| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `docs/content/docs/lume/guide/getting-started/*.mdx` | Rewritten quickstart, intro, install, FAQ |
| `docs/content/docs/lume/guide/fundamentals/*.mdx` | Rewritten VM management, unattended setup |
| `docs/content/docs/lume/guide/advanced/*.mdx` | Improved HTTP server, Lumier docs |
| `docs/content/docs/lume/examples/` | **New** - Claude Cowork sandbox example |
| `docs/public/img/lume-architecture.png` | **New** - Architecture diagram |
| `docs/content/docs/cuabench/examples/` | Reorganized examples |

### Other
| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `libs/lume/src/Resources/unattended-presets/sequoia.yml` | **New** - Sequoia preset |
| `libs/lume/src/Utils/CommandDocExtractor.swift` | Minor fix |
| `libs/lume/src/VM/VMDetailsPrinter.swift` | Minor fix |
| `docs/src/providers/copilotkit-provider.tsx` | Config update |

## Test plan

- [ ] Run `lume` and verify ASCII banner appears
- [ ] Run `LUME_TELEMETRY_DEBUG=on lume ls` and verify telemetry logs
- [ ] Run `lume config telemetry status` to check telemetry state
- [ ] Run `lume config telemetry disable` then verify events not sent
- [ ] Run `./scripts/build/build-debug.sh` from different directories
- [ ] Test uninstall script with `./scripts/uninstall.sh --help`
- [ ] Verify documentation renders correctly on docs site

The lume CLI requires `lume_lume.bundle` for unattended presets (sequoia, tahoe). Previously, only the binary was installed, causing this fatal error:

```
lume/resource_bundle_accessor.swift:12: Fatal error: could not load resource bundle: from /Users/.../.local/bin/lume_lume.bundle
```

- **build-release.sh**: Copy bundle to `.release/` and install alongside binary
- **build-release-notarized.sh**: Include bundle in release tarball

## Summary

Fix the unattended setup failure at command 170 where `<click 'Search'>` couldn't find the System Settings search field.

## Root Cause

The failure occurred because Terminal was still the active window when trying to interact with System Settings. The `<cmd+q>` to close Terminal and subsequent Spotlight search weren't reliable without proper keyboard focus.

## Changes

1. **Add desktop click** - Click at center of screen after closing Terminal to ensure keyboard focus
2. **Increase delays** - More time for app transitions (Terminal close, Spotlight, System Settings launch)
3. **Add verification wait** - `<wait 'General', timeout=30>` to confirm System Settings opened
4. **Use keyboard shortcut** - `<cmd+f>` instead of `<click 'Search'>` for reliable search field focus
5. **Increase toggle delay** - From 0.3s to 0.5s before toggling full disk access

## Affected Presets

- `sequoia.yml`
- `tahoe.yml`

- [ ] Verify full disk access toggle is enabled

## Summary

Fixes SSH availability detection in `lume ls` output. The issue was that `nc -G` (TCP connection timeout) doesn't work reliably with VM networking on macOS, but `-w` (overall timeout) does.

**Before:** `lume ls` shows `no` for SSH even when SSH is working
**After:** `lume ls` correctly shows SSH availability

## Testing

Verified manually:
- `nc -z -G 2 <vm-ip> 22` → fails with "Operation timed out"
- `nc -z -w 2 <vm-ip> 22` → succeeds with "Connection succeeded!"

sources.body:
## Summary

- **MCP Server**: Add native Model Context Protocol support via `lume serve --mcp` for AI agent integration (Claude Desktop, Claude Cowork)
- **Async VM Creation**: VM creation now returns immediately with 202 Accepted while provisioning runs in background
- **Provisioning Status**: New `provisioning` status with operation tracking (`ipsw_install`, `unattended_setup`) - works for both CLI and HTTP/MCP
- **MCP Tools**: Full VM management toolkit including `lume_create_vm`, `lume_list_vms`, `lume_get_vm`, `lume_run_vm`, `lume_stop_vm`, `lume_clone_vm`, `lume_delete_vm`, `lume_exec`
- **MCP Resources**: Documentation resources (`lume://usage-guide`, `lume://credentials`) for AI agent context
- **MCP Prompts**: Pre-defined workflows (`create-sandbox`, `run-in-sandbox`, `reset-sandbox`) for guided tasks
- **Documentation**: MCP server guide, updated HTTP API docs, FAQ with VM status explanations, nested virtualization FAQ
- **Examples Restructure**: Reorganized into Claude Code (SSH-based) and Claude Cowork (MCP-based) sections
- **Unattended Presets**: Updated to prevent screen lock/sleep during automated setup

## MCP Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      Your Mac (Host)                            │
│                                                                 │
│  ┌──────────────────┐           ┌───────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Claude Desktop   │◄── MCP ──►│ Lume MCP Server           │  │
│  │ or Claude Cowork │  (stdio)  │ (lume serve --mcp)        │  │
│  └──────────────────┘           └─────────────┬─────────────┘  │
│                                               │                 │
│                                               │ VM Control      │
│                                               ▼                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐│
│  │                   macOS VM Sandbox                          ││
│  │  Claude can: create, list, run, stop, clone, delete, exec   ││
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘│
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## MCP Capabilities

**Tools** - VM management operations:
- `lume_create_vm`, `lume_list_vms`, `lume_get_vm`
- `lume_run_vm`, `lume_stop_vm`, `lume_clone_vm`, `lume_delete_vm`
- `lume_exec` (SSH command execution)

**Resources** - Documentation for AI context:
- `lume://usage-guide` - Workflows, best practices, status reference
- `lume://credentials` - Default SSH credentials

**Prompts** - Guided workflows:
- `create-sandbox` - Create VM with unattended setup
- `run-in-sandbox` - Execute commands in VM
- `reset-sandbox` - Reset from golden image

## Provisioning Status

VMs now show their creation status immediately:

```
NAME      STATUS                           IP
sandbox   provisioning (ipsw_install)      -
dev-vm    running                          192.168.64.3
test      stopped                          -
```

Works for CLI, HTTP API, and MCP.

## Test plan

- [ ] Build lume with `swift build`
- [ ] Test MCP server: `npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector lume serve --mcp`
- [ ] Verify resources and prompts appear in MCP inspector
- [ ] Test `lume create` shows provisioning status in `lume ls`
- [ ] Test async VM creation via HTTP API returns 202 immediately
- [ ] Test MCP tools in Claude Desktop with configured `claude_desktop_config.json`
- [ ] Verify documentation renders correctly
sources.title: feat(lume): add MCP server with async VM creation and provisioning status

## Summary

Fix `lume ls` to show VMs with "provisioning" status immediately when `lume create` is running, and prevent lifecycle operations on provisioning VMs.

## Problem

1. When running `lume create`, the VM wasn't visible in `lume ls` until creation completed
2. Users could potentially attempt to run/stop/clone a VM that was still being provisioned

## Changes

### Show provisioning VMs in list (`VMDirectory.swift`)
- `initialized()` now returns true if config + provisioning marker exist (not just config + disk + nvram)
- VMs appear immediately with `provisioning (ipsw_install)` status during creation

### Block lifecycle operations on provisioning VMs (`LumeController.swift`, `Errors.swift`)
- Added `VMError.stillProvisioning` error case
- Added `validateNotProvisioning()` helper method
- `runVM`: Cannot start a VM that is still being provisioned
- `stopVM`: Cannot stop a VM that is still being provisioned
- `clone`: Cannot clone from a VM that is still being provisioned

### Cleanup
- Removed duplicate `createAsync`/`createInternal` functions from merge conflict

## Test plan

- [ ] Run `lume create test --os macos --ipsw latest`
- [ ] In another terminal, run `lume ls` immediately - verify VM shows with `provisioning (ipsw_install)`
- [ ] Try `lume run test` while provisioning - verify error: "Virtual machine is still being provisioned"
- [ ] Try `lume clone test test2` while provisioning - verify same error
sources.title: fix(lume): show provisioning status immediately in CLI lume ls

sources.body:
## Summary

Fix `lume ls` hanging when stale session files exist from crashed/killed lume processes.

## Problem

When lume crashes or is killed (`kill -9`) without proper cleanup, session files (`sessions.json`) are left behind. When `lume ls` runs, it sees these files, assumes the VM is running, and tries network operations (DHCP lookup, SSH check) that hang or timeout.

## Solution

Validate session files before assuming a VM is running:

1. Parse the VNC port from the session URL
2. Check if a process is actually listening on that port (using `lsof`)
3. If not, automatically clean up the stale session file

## Changes

- `NetworkUtils.swift`: Add `isLocalPortInUse()` function using `lsof`
- `LumeController.swift`: Add `parseVNCPort()` helper
- `LumeController.swift`: Validate sessions in `getVMDetailsLightweight()` before marking VM as running

## Test plan

1. Start a VM with `lume run`
2. Kill lume with `kill -9` (leaving stale session file)
3. Run `lume ls` - should show VM as "stopped" and log "Cleaned up stale session file"
sources.title: fix(lume): clean up stale session files on VM list

sources.body:
## Summary

Simplify provisioning state management and rename default storage.

### 1. Simplified Provisioning Markers

**Before:** Markers for both `ipsw_install` and `unattended_setup` phases
**After:** Markers only during IPSW install (before disk/nvram exist)

| Has disk + nvram? | Has marker? | Status |
|-------------------|-------------|--------|
| No | Yes | `provisioning (ipsw_install)` |
| Yes | Yes | Auto-cleanup marker, show normal status |
| Yes | No | `running` / `stopped` |

- During unattended setup, VM shows as **"running"** (it IS running)
- Auto-cleanup stale markers for complete VMs
- Stale warning (>8 hours) only for incomplete VMs

### 2. Rename Default Storage to "home"

- New installations use "home" instead of "default"
- Reflects the `~/.lume` location
- Non-breaking for existing configs

## Test plan

- [ ] `lume create --unattended` shows "provisioning" during IPSW, then "running" during setup
- [ ] Complete VMs with leftover markers → auto-cleaned
- [ ] Incomplete VMs stuck > 8 hours → "provisioning (stale)"
- [ ] New installation shows "home" storage
sources.title: feat(lume): add stale provisioning warning and rename default storage to home

sources.body:
## Summary

The MCP server's `handleRunVM` was directly awaiting `controller.runVM()` which blocks forever (VM.run has an infinite loop to keep the VM alive). This caused MCP clients to timeout waiting for a response.

## Changes

- Use `Task.detached` pattern (same as HTTP API in `Handlers.swift`) to run VM in background
- Wait 2 seconds for VM to initialize before returning
- Use `getDetails()` to get VM status including IP address

This matches the HTTP API behavior where the run call returns immediately while the VM continues running in the background.

## Test plan

- [ ] Test `lume run` via MCP server returns immediately
- [ ] Verify VM actually starts and runs
- [ ] Verify IP address is returned in response
sources.title: fix(lume): make MCP server runVM non-blocking

## Summary

The MCP server's `lume_get_vm` was using `controller.get().details` which doesn't include provisioning status. This caused provisioning VMs to show `status: "stopped"` instead of `status: "provisioning"`.

This is the same bug that was fixed in the CLI's `lume get` command in PR #853.

## Changes

- Use `controller.getDetails()` instead of `controller.get().details` in `handleGetVM`

## Test plan

- [ ] Create a VM via MCP (`lume_create_vm`)
- [ ] While provisioning, call `lume_get_vm` 
- [ ] Verify status shows "provisioning" (not "stopped")
- [ ] After provisioning completes, verify status shows "stopped"

sources.body:
## Summary

Multiple fixes for MCP and HTTP API server to correctly show VM provisioning status and avoid blocking.

## Changes

### 1. MCP server `runVM` non-blocking
- Use `Task.detached` pattern (same as HTTP API) to run VM in background
- MCP call now returns immediately instead of blocking forever
- Wait 2 seconds for VM to initialize before returning status

### 2. MCP server `lume_get_vm` provisioning status
- Use `controller.getDetails()` instead of `controller.get().details`
- Now correctly shows "provisioning" status for VMs being created

### 3. HTTP server `handleGetVM` provisioning status
- Same fix as MCP server - use `getDetails()` for correct status
- Cleaned up debug print statements

### 4. `createAsync` race condition fix
- Move provisioning marker creation out of `Task.detached` block
- VM now shows "provisioning" status immediately when `createAsync` returns
- Fixes race condition where `getDetails` briefly showed "stopped"

## Test plan

- [ ] Create VM via MCP - verify it returns immediately (not blocking)

## Summary
Adds a script to build and install lume from local source code instead of downloading from GitHub releases.

## Features
- Builds debug (default) or release configuration
- Codesigns binary with entitlements
- Installs to `~/.local/bin`
- Copies resource bundle (unattended presets)
- Sets up LaunchAgent daemon
- Skips auto-updater (not needed for local builds)

## Usage
```bash
# Build debug and install (default)
./libs/lume/scripts/install-local.sh

# Build release instead
./libs/lume/scripts/install-local.sh --release

# Custom port for daemon
./libs/lume/scripts/install-local.sh --port 7778

# Skip background service
./libs/lume/scripts/install-local.sh --no-background-service
```

## Test plan
- [ ] Run script and verify lume builds and installs
- [ ] Verify LaunchAgent starts correctly
- [ ] Test --release flag
- [ ] Test --no-background-service flag
sources.title: feat(lume): add install-local.sh script for local/debug builds

## Summary
- Updates lume CI/CD workflows to use Xcode 16.2 (Swift 6.2) instead of Xcode 16.0 (Swift 6.0)
- Fixes build failure for lume v0.2.53 caused by strict Sendable conformance checks in Swift 6.0

## Context
The `swift-nio-ssh` library's `NIOSSHHandler` is explicitly marked as non-Sendable, which causes concurrency violations when used across async boundaries. Swift 6.0 treats these as errors, while Swift 6.2 treats them as warnings.

Related: https://github.com/apple/swift-nio-ssh/issues/207

## Test plan
- [ ] CI build passes with Xcode 16.2
sources.title: fix(lume): use Xcode 16.2 (Swift 6.2) in CI workflows

sources.body:
## Summary

Adds automatic **bidirectional** clipboard synchronization between the macOS host and running Lume VMs via SSH.

- **Host → VM:** Copy text on your Mac, paste in the VM
- **VM → Host:** Copy text in the VM, paste on your Mac

### How it works
- `ClipboardWatcher` monitors both host clipboard (`NSPasteboard`) and VM clipboard (via `pbpaste` over SSH)
- Syncs changes in both directions with loop prevention
- Polling interval: 1 second
- Startup delay: 10 seconds (allows VM to boot and get IP)

### Requirements
- **SSH/Remote Login must be enabled on the VM**
- VMs created with `--unattended` have this enabled by default (user: `lume`, password: `lume`)
- For manual setup: System Settings → General → Sharing → Remote Login

### Features
- Bidirectional sync - works both ways
- Loop prevention - tracks last synced content to avoid ping-pong
- Silently skips if SSH not available (graceful degradation)
- 1MB size limit for clipboard content
- Base64 encoding for safe transport

## Test Plan
1. Create VM with `lume create test-vm --os macos --ipsw latest --unattended tahoe`
2. Run VM with `lume run test-vm`
3. Wait for VM to boot and complete setup (~10-15 min)
4. **Host → VM:** Copy text on host Mac, paste in VM Terminal
5. **VM → Host:** Copy text in VM, paste on host Mac

## Related
- Addresses #924 (VNC clipboard issue) - provides an alternative solution via SSH
- Reference: [Tart's guest agent approach](https://github.com/cirruslabs/tart/issues/14)
sources.title: feat(lume): add automatic clipboard sync from host to VM

## Summary

Fixes #923 - `--disk-size` now defaults to GB instead of MB when no unit is specified.

### Before
```bash
lume create my-vm --disk-size 50  # Created 50MB disk (unusable)
```

### After
```bash
lume create my-vm --disk-size 50   # Creates 50GB disk
lume create my-vm --disk-size 50GB # Creates 50GB disk
lume create my-vm --disk-size 51200MB # Creates 50GB disk
```

### Changes
1. **Default unit is now GB** - Numbers without units are treated as GB
2. **Sanity check added** - Throws error if disk is too small:
   - macOS VMs: minimum 30GB
   - Linux VMs: minimum 10GB
3. **Updated help text** - Clarifies that numbers without units are GB

### Files Changed
- `libs/lume/src/Utils/Utils.swift` - Changed default multiplier from MB to GB
- `libs/lume/src/Commands/Create.swift` - Added sanity check, updated help
- `libs/lume/src/Commands/Set.swift` - Updated help text

## Test Plan
```bash
# Should create 50GB disk
lume create test1 --os macos --ipsw latest --disk-size 50

# Should fail with "too small" error
lume create test2 --os macos --ipsw latest --disk-size 10

# Should work (explicit GB)
lume create test3 --os macos --ipsw latest --disk-size 50GB
```
sources.title: fix(lume): default disk/memory size to GB when no unit specified

## Summary

Fixes clipboard sync for large content (> 64KB) by using heredoc instead of command line arguments.

### Problem
When clipboard content is large, the base64-encoded string can exceed shell `ARG_MAX` limits when passed as a command line argument.

### Solution
- **Short content** (<64KB): Use inline `printf` command (fast)
- **Long content** (64KB-1MB): Use heredoc to pass content via stdin

### Changes
- Added conditional logic to use heredoc for large content
- Increased timeout from 5s to 10s for large transfers

## Test Plan
1. Copy a large text file (>64KB) to clipboard
2. Run VM with `lume run my-vm`
3. Verify clipboard syncs to VM without errors
sources.title: fix(lume): use heredoc for large clipboard content to avoid shell limits

## Summary
- The presets in `resources/` were **not being used** - only the ones in `src/Resources/unattended-presets/` are bundled via SwiftPM's `Bundle.module`
- This caused fixes from PRs #824 and #825 to not be included in releases
- Sync bundled presets with the latest fixes (System Settings navigation improvements)
- Remove duplicate files from `resources/` that were never used
- Fix usage comments to show preset names instead of file paths

## Root Cause
Package.swift declares resources as:
```swift
resources: [
    .copy("Resources/unattended-presets")
]
```
This bundles `src/Resources/unattended-presets/`, not `resources/`. When fixes were made in #824 and #825, they were applied to the wrong location.

## Test plan

- [ ] Run `lume create` with `--unattended sequoia` or `--unattended tahoe` and verify the preset works

## Summary
Adds commands to disable screen lock and sleep in unattended presets to prevent re-login prompts in VMs.

## Changes
Both `tahoe.yml` and `sequoia.yml` presets now include:

```yaml
# Disable screen saver
- defaults -currentHost write com.apple.screensaver idleTime -int 0

# Disable require password after sleep/screen saver
- defaults write com.apple.screensaver askForPassword -int 0

# Prevent display from sleeping (never)
- sudo pmset -a displaysleep 0

# Prevent system sleep (never)
- sudo pmset -a sleep 0

# Disable auto-logout
- sudo defaults write /Library/Preferences/.GlobalPreferences com.apple.autologout.AutoLogOutDelay -int 0
```

## Problem
VMs created with unattended setup would prompt for re-login after the screensaver activated, breaking automated workflows.

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify screensaver doesn't activate

sources.body:
## Summary
Follow-up to #1008 that keeps the original bridged-networking work intact and addresses issues found during review/testing.

This PR contains the original `#1008` commit plus two fix commits:
- `fix(lume): validate and persist network mode settings`
- `fix(lume): use local-safe entitlements for ad-hoc installs`

## Why this follow-up was needed
After pulling/testing `#1008`, there were functional regressions and one compile-time break:
- API run handler had a compile error due to missing `network` arg in `RunVMRequest` fallback init.
- VM creation paths could drop configured bridged mode and revert to NAT.
- `lume run` and API run/create paths could force NAT instead of respecting persisted VM config.
- API accepted invalid network strings and silently coerced them to NAT.
- Local debug installer signed ad-hoc binaries with `com.apple.vm.networking`, which can cause macOS to kill the binary at launch.

## What changed
### Networking correctness and validation
- Added strict request parsing helpers for network mode:
  - `RunVMRequest.parseNetworkMode()`
  - `CreateVMRequest.parseNetworkMode()`
- API handlers now validate `network` and return a bad request on invalid values (no silent NAT fallback).
- Fixed run-handler fallback initializer to include `network: nil`.

### Respect persisted VM network config
- `lume run --network` is now an optional override.
- If `--network` is omitted, run uses the VM’s configured network mode.
- `LumeController.runVM` now takes `networkMode: NetworkMode? = nil` and logs whether it is override vs config.

### Preserve network mode across VM creation/setup
- Threaded `networkMode` through temporary VM config creation.
- Ensured Darwin/Linux setup config rewrites preserve existing `vmDirContext.config.networkMode`.

### Local installer signing safety
- Added `libs/lume/resources/lume.local.entitlements` (virtualization-only entitlement for local ad-hoc installs).
- `scripts/install-local.sh` now:
  - signs with local-safe entitlements by default,
  - adds `--bridged-entitlement` opt-in for environments with proper Apple-approved signing,
  - falls back to local-safe entitlements if bridged-entitlement signed binary fails to launch,
  - documents this behavior in help/output.

## Behavior changes
- `lume run <vm>` without `--network` now uses the VM’s persisted network mode.
- API now rejects invalid network values instead of silently coercing them to NAT.
- Local `install-local.sh` defaults to non-bridged entitlement profile for launch reliability.

## Validation performed
- `swift build` in `libs/lume` (passes).
- `bash -n libs/lume/scripts/install-local.sh` (passes).
- `install-local.sh --help` output checked (new flag/help text present).
- Reproduced/verified launch behavior difference between ad-hoc signing with bridged entitlement vs local-safe entitlements.

## Attribution
Original bridged networking implementation remains attributed to `#1008` (`feat(lume): Add bridged networking support (--network bridged)`), preserved in this branch history.
sources.title: fix(lume): follow-up for bridged networking + local installer signing

sources.body:
## Summary
- Remove `com.apple.vm.networking` from `lume.entitlements` (used by release/notarized builds)

## Problem
The `com.apple.vm.networking` entitlement is a restricted entitlement that requires a matching provisioning profile. Without one, macOS's AMFI (Apple Mobile File Integrity) kills the binary immediately on launch with error `-413: No matching profile found` — even for commands like `lume ls` that don't use bridged networking.

This was introduced in the bridged networking PR and makes the v0.2.78 release completely unusable.

## Fix
Remove the entitlement from the release entitlements file. The entitlement remains in `lume.local.entitlements` for local development builds where users can provide their own provisioning profile.

## Test plan
- [ ] Build and sign with `lume.entitlements` — binary should launch without being killed
- [ ] `lume ls`, `lume run`, `lume serve` all work normally
- [ ] Bridged networking correctly errors when attempted (entitlement not present)
sources.title: fix(lume): remove com.apple.vm.networking from release entitlements

sources.body:
## Summary
Fix noisy clipboard sync error logs that flood console output when SSH connectivity to the VM is disrupted.

Closes #1054

## Problem
The clipboard watcher polls every 1 second via SSH. When the SSH connection fails (e.g., `NIOCore.ChannelError error 9`), it logs `Failed to sync clipboard from VM` on every single poll cycle, flooding the user's console with identical error messages.

## Changes
- **Error suppression**: Log the first occurrence of an error, then suppress identical consecutive errors after 3 failures. Log once when suppression kicks in.
- **Backoff**: Increase poll interval from 1s to 5s when SSH is consistently failing, reducing resource waste.
- **SSH client caching**: Reuse the SSH client across poll cycles instead of creating a new connection every second. Invalidate on error so reconnection happens on the next cycle.
- **Recovery logging**: Log when sync recovers after a period of suppressed errors.

## Behavior
Before: `Failed to sync clipboard from VM` printed every second indefinitely.

After:
```
[...] DEBUG: Failed to sync clipboard from VM vm=myvm error=...ChannelError error 9.
[...] DEBUG: Failed to sync clipboard from VM vm=myvm error=...ChannelError error 9.
[...] DEBUG: Failed to sync clipboard from VM vm=myvm error=...ChannelError error 9.
[...] DEBUG: Clipboard sync errors repeating, suppressing further logs until resolved vm=myvm
(silence until resolved or error type changes)
[...] DEBUG: Clipboard sync recovered vm=myvm
```

## Test plan
- [ ] `swift build` passes
- [ ] Run a VM, verify clipboard sync works normally
- [ ] Kill SSH on the VM, verify error logs are suppressed after 3 occurrences
- [ ] Re-enable SSH, verify "recovered" message appears and sync resumes
sources.title: fix(lume): suppress repetitive clipboard sync error logs

sources.body:
## Summary

Restructures the lume release binary as a `.app` bundle to support macOS bridged networking via the `com.apple.vm.networking` entitlement. This restricted entitlement requires a provisioning profile, which in turn requires a proper `.app` bundle structure.

### What changed

- **`.app` bundle assembly** in all three build scripts (`build-release-notarized.sh`, `build-release.sh`, `install-local.sh`):
  - Binary placed at `Contents/MacOS/lume`
  - `Info.plist` stamped with version at `Contents/Info.plist`
  - Provisioning profile embedded at `Contents/embedded.provisionprofile`
  - SPM resource bundle (`lume_lume.bundle`) placed in `Contents/Resources/`
  - Backward-compatible wrapper script (`#!/bin/sh exec .../lume.app/Contents/MacOS/lume "$@"`)

- **Codesign fixes** in `build-release-notarized.sh`:
  - Added `--options runtime` for hardened runtime (required for notarization)
  - Added `--timestamp` for secure timestamps (required for notarization)
  - Fixed `--entitlements` flag (was singular `--entitlement`)
  - Added `--keychain` flag + keychain search list setup for CI
  - Added `codesign -dvv` verification step after signing
  - Uses `ditto` instead of `cp -R` for pkg staging (preserves signatures)

- **Notarization error reporting**: Added `notarytool log` fetching on failure to capture Apple's detailed error JSON

- **Bundle.module resolution fix**: SPM's auto-generated `Bundle.module` looks at `Bundle.main.bundleURL` (the `.app` root), but:
  - Placing the resource bundle at the `.app` root fails codesign ("unsealed contents")
  - Placing it in `Contents/MacOS/` fails codesign ("bundle format unrecognized")
  - Only `Contents/Resources/` works for codesign, but `Bundle.module` doesn't check there
  - **Solution**: Added `Bundle.lumeResources` custom accessor that checks `resourceURL` first (`.app` bundles) then `bundleURL` (standalone), replacing all `Bundle.module` usage in `UnattendedConfig.swift`

- **`.gitignore`**: Added `*.provisionprofile`

### CI verification

- Run [22439923600](https://github.com/trycua/cua/actions/runs/22439923600): Build + codesign + notarize all pass
  - `CodeDirectory flags=0x10000(runtime)` — hardened runtime
  - `Authority=Developer ID Application` — proper certificate chain
  - `Timestamp` — secure timestamp present
  - Notarization: **Accepted**

### Required CI secrets

- `PROVISIONING_PROFILE_BASE64` — Base64-encoded Developer ID provisioning profile with `com.apple.vm.networking` entitlement for bundle ID `com.trycua.lume`

sources.body:
## Summary

- **Prevent `lume ls` hangs**: Added `runWithTimeout` helper in `NetworkUtils` that kills stuck subprocesses (`lsof`, `nc`, `ping`) after a deadline. Previously, `Process.waitUntilExit()` had no timeout, so `lume ls` could hang indefinitely when subprocesses got stuck during concurrent VM state transitions (e.g., running `lume ls` while `lume stop` is tearing down networking).

- **SSH system fallback**: Added `SystemSSHClient` that delegates to `/usr/bin/ssh` with `SSH_ASKPASS` for password authentication. `lume ssh` now tries NIO SSH first and silently falls back to system SSH on connection failure. This fixes SSH in sandboxed/restricted environments where macOS blocks non-system-signed binaries from making direct TCP connections to vmnet interfaces (errno 65: EHOSTUNREACH).

- **Bridged mode IP resolution**: Added ARP table lookup as fallback in `DHCPLeaseParser`. In bridged mode, VMs get their IP from the physical network's DHCP server (not vmnet), so `lume ls`/`lume get` showed a stale NAT IP. Now checks the ARP table first (recent, accurate for bridged) and falls back to vmnet DHCP leases (for NAT mode). NAT traffic doesn't appear in the host ARP table, so there's no ambiguity.

## Files changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `libs/lume/src/Utils/NetworkUtils.swift` | `runWithTimeout` helper, kill timeouts on all subprocess calls |
| `libs/lume/src/SSH/SystemSSHClient.swift` | **New** — system SSH client using `/usr/bin/ssh` + `SSH_ASKPASS` |
| `libs/lume/src/Commands/SSH.swift` | NIO → system SSH fallback on connection failure |
| `libs/lume/src/Virtualization/DHCPLeaseParser.swift` | ARP table lookup for bridged mode IP resolution |

## Test plan

- [x] `lume ls` no longer hangs during concurrent `lume stop` operations
- [x] `lume ssh` works in restricted environments (falls back to system SSH)
- [x] `lume ls` shows correct bridged IP (192.168.14.x) instead of stale NAT IP (192.168.64.x)
- [x] `lume ssh` still works in NAT mode
- [x] Bridged networking verified end-to-end: VM gets IP on host subnet, SSH works, internet works
- [ ] CI build passes

sources.body:
## Summary

- Use `.cached` caching mode for virtio disk attachments in Linux VMs instead of `.automatic`
- Add optional `cachingMode` parameter to `createStorageDeviceConfiguration` and `createUSBMassStorageDeviceConfiguration` (defaults to `.automatic` to preserve macOS VM behavior)

Fixes #1099

## Context

Apple's Virtualization framework has a known issue where the default `.automatic` caching mode for `VZDiskImageStorageDeviceAttachment` causes EXT4/filesystem corruption in Linux guest VMs. This has been independently discovered and fixed by both UTM and Lima:

- **UTM**: https://github.com/utmapp/UTM/pull/5919
- **Lima**: https://github.com/lima-vm/lima/pull/2026

Symptoms include EXT4 metadata checksum errors, read-only filesystem remounts, and data loss — particularly on VMs stored on external drives.

The fix sets `cachingMode: .cached` for Linux VMs only. macOS VMs continue to use `.automatic` and are unaffected.

## Test plan

- [ ] Create a new Linux VM with `lume create`
- [ ] Run the VM and verify no EXT4 checksum errors in `dmesg`
- [ ] Clone the VM and verify the clone boots cleanly
- [ ] Run heavy I/O workload (e.g., `dd`, large compilation) and verify no corruption
- [ ] Verify macOS VMs are unaffected (still use `.automatic`)

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
