sources.body:
## Summary

Adds a cross-platform VNC backend to the computer-server as an alternative to native OS-specific handlers. The VNC backend connects to any VNC server to perform screenshots, mouse, keyboard, and scroll operations over the RFB protocol — bypassing local TCC permissions and working across macOS, Linux, and Windows targets.

### Key changes

- **VNC automation handler** (`handlers/vnc.py`) built on [vncdotool](https://github.com/sibson/vncdotool) — Twisted-based RFB client bridged to asyncio via `asyncio.to_thread`
- **`--backend native|vnc` CLI flag** — selects handler backend at startup (default: `native`)
- **`--vnc-host`, `--vnc-port`, `--vnc-password` CLI args** — VNC connection config
- **`CUA_BACKEND` / `CUA_VNC_HOST` / `CUA_VNC_PORT` / `CUA_VNC_PASSWORD` env vars** — alternative to CLI flags
- **`backend`, `vnc_host`, `vnc_port`, `vnc_password` params on `Computer()` client** — pass backend config through the client API, flows through `run_opts["env"]` for VM providers
- **Deduplicated shared handlers** — `run_command`, `copy_to_clipboard`, `set_clipboard` moved to `BaseAutomationHandler` as concrete methods inherited by all backends (-186 lines, +90 lines net)
- **`vncdotool>=1.2.0`** added as optional dependency (`pip install cua-computer-server[vnc]`)

### What works over VNC

| Operation | Method |
|-----------|--------|
| Screenshots | VNC framebuffer (`refreshScreen`) |
| Mouse (click, drag, scroll) | VNC pointer events |
| Keyboard (type, hotkey, press) | VNC key events |
| Clipboard read/write | `pbcopy`/`pbpaste` (macOS) or `pyperclip` (inherited from base) |
| Shell commands | Local `asyncio.create_subprocess_shell` (server runs inside VM) |
| Accessibility tree | Stub (not available over VNC) |

### Usage

```bash
# CLI
cua-computer-server --backend vnc --vnc-host 192.168.64.1 --vnc-port 5900 --vnc-password secret

# Env vars
CUA_BACKEND=vnc CUA_VNC_HOST=192.168.64.1 CUA_VNC_PORT=5900 python -m computer_server

# Python client
computer = Computer(backend="vnc", vnc_host="192.168.64.1", vnc_port=5900, vnc_password="secret")
```

## Test plan

- [x] macOS Tahoe VM (VNC backend via Lume) — 18/18 operations pass
- [x] Linux cua-xfce container (native backend) — 14/14 operations pass
- [x] Clipboard round-trip verified (set → read back)
- [x] `run_command` verified (whoami, uname, file I/O)
- [x] Screenshot captures verified visually
- [ ] Windows handler (not tested, but only clipboard dedup — `run_command` override preserved)
sources.title: feat(computer-server): add VNC backend with --backend flag

sources.body:
## Summary

- **VNC backend rewrite** (`vnc.py`): Replaced `vncdotool.api.connect()` (which deadlocks inside uvicorn/asyncio on Python 3.13) with a global Twisted reactor pattern. Each VNC operation creates a fresh connection via `reactor.callFromThread()` + `threading.Event` synchronization. All closures use `@defer.inlineCallbacks` / `yield`. Also fixes `mouseDrag` (`doPoll` not available on `SelectReactor`) by using manual `mouseMove` increments, and fixes `client.screen` access.
- **VirtioFS port discovery** (`VM.swift`, `Run.swift`, `LumeController.swift`, `VNCService.swift`): Lume now creates a temp "lume-config" VirtioFS shared directory, writes `vnc.env` (dynamic port + password) after VNC starts. The guest mounts the share and reads the config on boot — no more hardcoded VNC port/password. Includes a VNC URL parsing fix (`URLComponents` with `vnc://` → `http://` replacement) since Swift's `URL` can't parse the `vnc://` scheme.
- **CLI env var fix** (`cli.py`): `CUA_VNC_PORT` is now only overridden from CLI args when explicitly provided, preventing the default (5900) from clobbering the env var set by the guest's `start_server.sh`.
- **Setup script update** (`setup-cua.sh`): Renamed from `setup-cua-computer.sh`. Added "already mounted" check (`mount | grep -q "lume-config"`) so `mount_virtiofs` doesn't fail on server restarts. Moved `vnc.env` wait loop outside the mount if/else so it runs regardless of mount state.

## Test plan

- [x] E2E test suite: 19/19 operations passing (screenshot, left/right/double click, key press, hotkey, type text, scroll up/down/generic, move cursor, get cursor position, get screen size, rapid screenshots, click+screenshot, drag, type/key aliases)

- [ ] Verify `setup-cua.sh` provisions a new VM correctly
- [ ] Verify fallback to hardcoded defaults when VirtioFS share is unavailable

## Summary

- **Auto-generated VNC credentials**: Lume generates a random port + 4-word passphrase for each VM's VNC server (no more hardcoded `--vnc-password lume`)
- **SSH-based VNC config delivery**: After VM boot, lume automatically writes `~/.vnc.env` inside the VM via SSH, bypassing macOS TCC restrictions that block VirtioFS reads from LaunchAgent processes
- **VNC backend fixes**: Fixed coordinate corruption bug (fresh connection per operation), fixed scroll direction, fixed connection leak
- **setup-cua.sh cleanup**: Removed all hardcoded VNC password/port defaults; websockify and start_server.sh now read from `~/.vnc.env`
- **CI fixes**: isort ordering, black formatting, MockVM signature, doc sync

## Details

macOS TCC blocks LaunchAgent processes from reading VirtioFS mounts (`Operation not permitted`), so the previous VirtioFS-only approach for delivering VNC config to guest VMs didn't work reliably. The new approach:

1. Lume writes `~/.vnc.env` via SSH after VM boot (background task, 6 retries × 5s, silent on failure)
2. VirtioFS remains as a fallback for interactive sessions
3. `start_server.sh` and `websockify` both read from `~/.vnc.env`

Each VM gets a unique random VNC port + password, ensuring VMs can't access each other's VNC servers.

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify websockify reads from `~/.vnc.env` correctly

sources.body:
## Summary

- OCI-format images bypassed the legacy `validateCache`/`copyFromCache` path entirely, causing every `lume pull` to re-download all layers from the registry even when nothing had changed.
- This PR adds per-layer content-addressed caching to `pullOCI`, matching the behaviour of the existing legacy pull path.
- Two new helpers (`validateOCICache` / `copyFromOCICache`) provide a top-level short-circuit: when every layer for the requested manifest digest is already in the local cache the entire network round-trip is skipped and the VM directory is assembled purely from cached files.

## What changed

- `pullOCI` now accepts a `manifestId` parameter (derived from the manifest digest via the existing `getManifestIdentifier` helper).
- Before downloading each layer (config blob, nvram blob, disk chunks, single disk blob) the code checks `getCachedLayerPath(manifestId:digest:)`; if the file exists it is reused instead of re-fetching.
- After each download the layer file is copied into the cache directory so future pulls can skip it.
- Cached chunk files are **not** deleted after decompression (freshly-downloaded temp files still are).
- All cache operations are guarded by the existing `cachingEnabled` property — behaviour is unchanged when caching is disabled.

## Why this matters

OCI layers are content-addressed by digest. The same image always produces the same set of digests, making the digest an ideal cache key. After the first pull, subsequent pulls of the same image are a fast local copy rather than a full download.

## Test plan

- [ ] Pull an OCI image once; verify all layers are downloaded and the VM boots.
- [ ] Pull the same OCI image again; verify the log shows "found in cache, skipping download" for each layer and no network traffic occurs.
- [ ] Disable caching (`LUME_CACHING_ENABLED=false` or equivalent) and verify a full download still occurs.
- [ ] Pull a different OCI image tag; verify a fresh download is triggered (different digest = different cache key).
- [ ] Verify chunked (multi-part) disk images are correctly reassembled from cache on the second pull.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

* **New Features**
  * Added container image layer caching to accelerate pulls and reduce bandwidth consumption.
  * Implemented cache-first retrieval logic that skips downloads when layers are already present locally.

sources.body:
## Problem

Lume's custom TCP server read requests with `minimumIncompleteLength: 1`, which fires as soon as **any** byte arrives. On a fresh TCP connection, HTTP headers and body can arrive in separate TCP segments. When this happens the server processes only the headers, body is `nil`, and returns 400 `Invalid request body` — even though the client sent a valid request.

This caused spurious failures on:
- `POST /lume/pull/start`
- `POST /lume/vms/clone`
- Any other body-bearing endpoint when the client opens a fresh TCP connection

The failure was non-deterministic (depends on TCP segment timing) which made it hard to reproduce consistently.

## Fix

Replace the hand-rolled `NWListener` / `NWConnection` server in `Server.swift` with SwiftNIO's `ServerBootstrap` + `NIOHTTP1` pipeline (`configureHTTPServerPipeline()`).

NIOHTTP1's built-in decoder accumulates `.head` + `.body` parts until the `.end` marker arrives — honoring `Content-Length` and chunked transfer encoding — then delivers a fully reassembled request to the handler. The partial-read race is eliminated at the library level.

## Changes

- **`Server.swift`**: Full rewrite using `ServerBootstrap` + `NIOHTTP1`. Routes and handlers are unchanged. Async dispatch bridges back to the NIO event loop via `EventLoopPromise` so `ChannelHandlerContext` (non-Sendable) never crosses actor boundaries.
- **`HTTP.swift`**: Add memberwise `init(method:path:headers:body:)` to `HTTPRequest` — the NIO handler receives parsed components directly, no raw-byte re-parsing.
- **`Package.swift`**: Add `NIOHTTP1` product (already in `swift-nio` dependency). Bump `swift-sdk` (MCP) `0.10.0` → `0.12.0` to fix a Swift 6 data race in `NetworkTransport` that broke clean builds under Swift 6.3.

## Test plan

- [ ] `swift build -c release` succeeds cleanly
- [ ] `POST /lume/pull/start` with a JSON body no longer returns `Invalid request body` on fresh connections
- [ ] `POST /lume/vms/clone` works reliably
- [ ] All existing lume CLI commands (`run`, `stop`, `delete`, `list`, etc.) continue to work
- [ ] Release workflow produces a notarized binary with the virtualization entitlement

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

## Summary
- **Auto-generated reference docs**: `scripts/gen_interface_docs.py` introspects all interface classes and emits `interfaces.mdx` with proper MDX escaping (HTML entities for `<>{}` outside code spans)
- **Comprehensive test coverage**: integration tests for every Image builder method (apt, pip, env, run, copy, expose, from_registry, from_file, apk_install, pwa_install) and every sandbox interface (shell, mouse, keyboard, screen, clipboard, tunnel, terminal, window, mobile)

sources.body:
## Problem

Fixes #296 — `lume pull` for the `macos-sequoia` images stalls around 30–40% and then enters an endless "Retrying download" loop with failing sha256 checksums.

## Root cause

Two issues in `downloadLayer` (`libs/lume/src/ContainerRegistry/ImageContainerRegistry.swift`):

1. **No integrity check.** Downloaded blobs were never verified against their content-addressed digest, so corrupt/truncated bytes were silently accepted, **written to the layer cache**, and reused on every subsequent pull — which is what makes the failure persist across re-pulls and "loop".

2. **Transport compression on content-addressed blobs.** The request sent `Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate`. OCI/Docker digests are computed over the exact stored bytes (already gzip/lz4-compressed), so when the CDN applied transport gzip, `URLSession` *transparently decompressed* the response before writing the file. The bytes on disk no longer matched the digest, and `expectedContentLength` became unreliable (chunked/`-1`), which is why progress appeared to freeze around 30%.

## Fix

- Request `Accept-Encoding: identity` so content-addressed blobs are never transparently decompressed.
- Stream-hash each downloaded layer (4 MiB chunks, so multi-GB disk layers aren't loaded into memory) and compare it to the expected `sha256:` digest **before** accepting it. On mismatch, delete the bad bytes (cache is never poisoned), route back through the existing bounded retry loop, and on persistent failure throw a clear, actionable `PullError.layerVerificationFailed` instead of producing a corrupt VM.
- Add a distinct log line for a real checksum mismatch so it's no longer indistinguishable from a generic network retry.

## Tests

- Added `tests/SHA256FileTests.swift` covering the streaming file-hash helper (known vectors, cross-chunk-boundary equivalence with the in-memory hash, and distinctness).
- `swift build` succeeds; the new tests pass:

```
✔ Suite SHA256FileTests passed after 0.033 seconds.
✔ Test run with 4 tests in 1 suite passed.
```

## Notes

This makes corruption (server-side or in-transit) **fail loudly and boundedly** rather than silently caching bad layers. If the registry blobs themselves are corrupt, users now get an explicit checksum error naming the expected vs. actual digest instead of an opaque infinite loop.

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

## Summary by CodeRabbit

## Release Notes

* **New Features**
  * Added SHA-256 checksum verification for downloaded container image layers to ensure download integrity.
  * Corrupted or invalid layers are automatically detected and removed.
  * Enhanced error messaging provides clear feedback when layer verification fails.

<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

sources.body:
## Summary

- add `lume sip on|off <vm>` for stopped macOS VMs
- preflight guest credentials over SSH, drive paired Recovery through VNC, and verify the exact `csrutil status` after a normal boot
- keep the guest admin password out of child argv with `--admin-password-stdin` and `vncdotool typefile -`
- add OCR checks for the selected admin account, confirmation/direct-password prompt variants, authentication failures, and the final SIP state
- harden child lifecycle handling with random effective VNC credentials, heartbeat checks, private file-backed logs, bounded helper processes, exact-child cleanup, and terminal-state assertions
- add an optional serial console for VM diagnostics
- document SIP using Diátaxis: a task-focused how-to, a LocalPolicy concept page, generated CLI reference, and concise limits reference
- enforce CLI documentation coverage against the command registry and fail generation when a command has no reference category

## Why

On Apple silicon VMs, SIP is represented by a signed LocalPolicy in paired auxiliary storage. It cannot be changed by editing `disk.img` or `nvram.bin` offline. Recovery is the supported place to run `csrutil`, so Lume needs to automate that flow and then independently prove the resulting state.

Tahoe Recovery does not always halt after `shutdown -h now`. The command gives it a bounded grace period, stops only that exact `lume run` child if necessary, waits for the VM to report `stopped`, and still requires an exact normal-boot SSH verification before reporting success.

## Validation

- `swift build --package-path libs/lume`
- `swift test`: 28 tests passed
- targeted Prettier check for new prose and generator source
- docs hygiene check
- docs link check: 0 errors
- generated-reference drift check
- production docs build
- locally signed Tahoe E2E:
  - `lume sip on macos-tahoe-recovery-data-test --yes --admin-password-stdin` verified `System Integrity Protection status: enabled.`
  - `lume sip off macos-tahoe-recovery-data-test --yes --admin-password-stdin` verified `System Integrity Protection status: disabled.`

`vncdotool` remains an optional dependency for this command. The in-tree native VNC client needs a separate connection/cancellation hardening pass before it can safely replace the proven CLI path.
sources.title: feat(lume): automate SIP changes through macOS Recovery

## Summary

- refresh APFS Preboot metadata after unattended guest finalization so the offline-created `lume` administrator is authorized in paired Recovery
- add a regression test for the exact finalization script sent over SSH
- make installer color setup tolerate missing terminal capabilities instead of exiting under `set -e`

## Release E2E finding

A clean Lume 0.3.13 test from the Tahoe 26.5.2 IPSW completed unattended setup successfully, but the first `lume sip off` attempt failed in Recovery with:

```text
csrutil: no admin users authorized for recovery
Authorized user:
```

The guest already showed `lume` as secure-token enabled and the APFS volume owner. Running `diskutil apfs updatePreboot /` generated the Recovery admin list and `AdminUserRecoveryInfo.plist`; the next `lume sip off` run completed and a separate reboot verified `System Integrity Protection status: disabled.`

The same E2E also found that `install.sh` exits before argument parsing when `tput` fails in a non-TTY shell. Empty color fallbacks match the existing uninstaller behavior.

## Validation

- fresh Tahoe IPSW install with `--unattended tahoe`
- guest checks: `lume` admin, autologin, Setup Assistant complete, Remote Login on
- pre-fix Recovery failure reproduced
- `diskutil apfs updatePreboot /` repair verified
- `lume sip off` completed after repair
- independent normal boot and SSH `csrutil status` verification passed
- `swift test`: 29 tests passed
- `bash -n libs/lume/scripts/install.sh`
- `env -u TERM ./libs/lume/scripts/install.sh --help`
- `git diff --check`

sources.body:
## Summary

- add recovery-preserving offline expansion for macOS VM disks
- expose resize controls through the CLI, HTTP API, and MCP server
- add transactional rollback, operation locking, layout validation, and APFS verification
- document the workflow using Diátaxis: a task-focused how-to, an explanation of the disk layout, and reference updates

## Why

A macOS VM disk cannot be safely expanded by only appending space: its paired Recovery partition sits after the main APFS container. Lume now relocates Recovery byte-for-byte, rewrites both GPT copies, grows APFS, verifies the result, and preserves the VM identity and boot-policy state.

## Validation

- `swift test` (59 tests)
- generated Lume CLI and HTTP reference drift check
- docs link and public-hygiene checks
- docs production build (63 pages)
- live disposable Tahoe VM expanded from 50 GB to 60 GB and then 70 GB
- confirmed the root/APFS capacity grew, paired Recovery remained available in the boot picker, SIP state was unchanged, and `softwareupdate --list` completed normally

## Remaining live-test caveat

No macOS update was available on the disposable VM, so update discovery was verified but an actual post-resize update installation was not exercised.
sources.title: feat(lume): expand macOS disks safely

## Summary

- default new macOS VM disks to 100GB in the CLI
- align the macOS-only MCP create tool with the same 100GB default
- preserve the existing 50GB Linux default and explicit disk-size overrides
- update generated command metadata and add regression coverage

## Why

The macOS VM creation defaults were inconsistent: the CLI used 50GB for every guest OS, while the MCP create tool used 64GB. A 100GB macOS default gives new VMs more practical headroom while retaining sparse-disk allocation behavior.

## Impact

New macOS VMs created without an explicit disk size receive a 100GB virtual disk. Linux defaults and explicit disk-size choices are unchanged.

## Validation

- rebased cleanly onto the latest `origin/main`
- `swift test` (61 tests passed)

sources.body:
## Problem
`lume sip off/on` drives `csrutil` in the guest's paired Recovery over VNC. On **macOS 26 (Tahoe)**, `csrutil disable`/`enable` changed its prompt flow: after the `[y/n]` confirmation it first asks for an **authorized administrator username** (`authorized user:`) and *then* a password, instead of going straight to `enter password for user <admin>:`.

The automation only matched the legacy password prompt, so on macOS 26 guests it answered `[y/n]` with `y` and then timed out:

```
Error: VNC driver timeout: Framebuffer for 'csrutil account prompt' did not contain expected text;
last OCR text: ... [y/n]: y authorized user: ...
```

## Fix
Handle both prompt styles. After the `[y/n]` confirmation, wait for either the legacy `enter password for user <admin>:` prompt **or** the new `authorized user` username prompt; when the username prompt appears, type the admin user, then wait for the password prompt before entering the password. Adds `06-account-prompt` / `06b-password-prompt` screenshots for debugging.

## Testing
Verified end-to-end on a macOS 26.4 guest: `lume sip off <vm>` now completes through the `authorized user:` → password flow and the follow-up normal boot reports `System Integrity Protection status: disabled.` (`SIP is disabled.`). Backward-compatible with older guests that use the single password prompt.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
sources.title: fix(lume): handle macOS 26 authorized-user prompt in SIP automation

sources.body:
## Summary

- Add default-on, consent-aware telemetry to Cua Driver and Lume with persistent disable controls and explicit reset and purge behavior.
- Send a content-free schema v2 envelope to PostHog EU with person profiles and GeoIP enrichment disabled.
- Record bounded CLI, MCP session, MCP tool outcome, install, release, and Lume usage events.
- Accept optional MCP agent context only through the documented `_meta["ai.cua/agent-context"]` object, then normalize provider and model values through fixed allowlists.
- Serialize installation identity and lifecycle state across processes, preserve returning identities during legacy-home migration, and retry failed lifecycle delivery after a 15-minute backoff.
- Persist installer attribution before binary activation so an immediate first command cannot mislabel an install as `first_run`.
- Publish the exact Cua Driver event schema and privacy boundaries.

## Privacy and user impact

A normal uninstall preserves the pseudonymous installation ID and saved preference so returning installations remain measurable. Disabling telemetry stops all telemetry requests. A first run with telemetry disabled creates no ID or markers. Reset and purge commands delete canonical and migratable legacy identity state.

Routine events exclude prompts, tool arguments and bodies, typed text, screenshots, accessibility trees, titles, application names, file paths, URLs, arbitrary metadata, and raw errors.

## Coordinated rollout

Cloud dashboards and GitHub collector: https://github.com/trycua/cloud/pull/5670

Apply the Cloud PR before releasing these clients. The trajectory-donation work remains separate.

## Checks

- `cargo test -p cua-driver --bin cua-driver --locked` (80 tests)
- `cargo test -p cua-driver-core --locked` (128 unit tests and 3 integration tests)
- `cargo check -p cua-driver --all-targets --locked`
- Focused telemetry tests (20), including multi-process identity/lifecycle, legacy reset, retry, and installer-attribution coverage
- Release wiring tests (10)
- Lume telemetry tests (6) and version-check tests (2)
- Python wrapper tests (3 passed, 3 platform skips)
- Shell syntax, documentation formatting, and git whitespace checks
- Independent privacy, concurrency, migration, and correctness review
sources.title: Add consent-aware telemetry for Cua Driver and Lume
